---
title: "Tool use & agents"
subtitle: "When the model stops just talking and starts doing"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Learning objectives
- Explain what "tool use" (a.k.a. function calling) means and when it changes the failure surface.
- Distinguish chat assistants, coding assistants, and autonomous agents.
- Reason about the marginal risks of giving an AI write access to your filesystem, shell, or APIs.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Page is conceptual; specific tools change rapidly
The agentic-AI landscape moves on a 6-month cycle. Specific products, APIs, and capability claims here will be out of date soon. Treat the **categories and risk patterns** as durable; verify any specific tool claim before relying on it.
:::

## Tool use in one paragraph

Modern LLMs can be given access to *tools* — functions they can call during a conversation (e.g., run a shell command, query a database, fetch a URL). The model decides when to call a tool, constructs the call, receives the result, and continues. This moves the system from "text predictor" to "loop that can act on the world."

## The spectrum (by blast radius)

- **Chat assistant** (pure text) — low blast radius. Failure = a bad paragraph. Mitigation = read it.
- **Coding assistant with file edits** (Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot, similar) — moderate blast radius. Failure = bad diffs, broken local state. Mitigation = human review before commit, version control, sandboxed checkout.
- **Agentic assistant with shell + network** — large blast radius. Failure = destructive commands, leaked credentials, runaway loops, exfiltration. Mitigation = sandboxing, scoped permissions, explicit confirmations, dry-run modes, audit logs.

The blast-radius framing matters more than the marketing label. A "chat assistant" with a single shell tool is functionally an agent.

## Implications for research workflows

- For analysis tasks, a coding assistant that reads/writes files is often the sweet spot — high productivity, low surprise surface.
- For long-running literature or data-collection tasks, agentic setups are tempting — but verify at every boundary, and prefer human-in-the-loop confirmation for irreversible actions.
- Never give an agent credentials it doesn't need. Never give an agent credentials whose blast radius you can't bound.
- Treat any agent with write access to a shared system (cloud bucket, lab database, version-controlled repo with auto-deploy) as a junior collaborator who never sleeps, has no judgment, and submits to whoever's prompt is loudest.

## Worked example: a minimal agent for bibliography cleanup

A postdoc has a `.bib` file with 80 entries, many with incorrect or missing DOIs, inconsistent journal name abbreviations, and wrong publication years. She wants to verify and fix each entry against CrossRef. The task is tedious by hand; the AI can automate the lookups. This is an agent: it reads a file, calls an external API in a loop, and proposes writes.

Here is the loop, described at the level of what the human approves:

1. **Read** — load `references.bib`, parse into a list of entries.
2. **For each entry** — send the title + first author to the CrossRef `/works` endpoint; retrieve the best match above a similarity threshold.
3. **Propose** — surface the diff (current vs. CrossRef) to the human: `DOI: missing → 10.1093/nar/gkab1049`, `year: 2020 → 2021`.
4. **Human confirms** — the human reviews each proposed change and types `y` or `n` (or `skip` for the whole entry).
5. **Write** — only after explicit confirmation, overwrite the entry in the `.bib` file.

The key design decision is step 4. Without a human-confirm gate, the agent would:

- silently correct entries that CrossRef matched wrong (common for short titles or names shared across multiple papers),
- overwrite your canonical abbreviation choices with CrossRef's,
- and propagate errors faster than you can track them.

The confirm gate converts an irreversible bulk operation into a reviewed sequence of small, reversible decisions. The agent's value is not autonomy — it is eliminating the tedious API lookup, leaving only judgment calls.

What this example is **not**: an argument that you should build this agent. A competent implementation requires error handling for CrossRef rate limits, disambiguation logic for multi-match results, and a rollback mechanism. For 80 entries, a careful manual pass is defensible. The point is the pattern — tool use becomes an agent when the model calls tools in a loop, and the critical risk control is the human gate before any write.

## Further reading

- Anthropic. (2025). [Building effective agents](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents). — Practical taxonomy of agentic patterns (augmented LLM, routing, parallelization, orchestration); the blast-radius framing maps directly onto the orchestrator/subagent distinction.
- Weng, L. (2023). [LLM-powered autonomous agents](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-06-23-agent/). *Lilian Weng's blog* — thorough technical walkthrough of memory, tool use, and planning in agent architectures.
- Yao, S., et al. (2023). [ReAct: Synergizing reasoning and acting in language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629). *ICLR 2023* — the paper that established the think → act → observe loop underlying most current agent frameworks.
- Ruan, Y., et al. (2023). [Identifying the risks of LM agents with an LM-emulated sandbox](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.15817). arXiv:2309.15817 — empirical study of agent failure modes; good grounding for the blast-radius framing above.
